rationale: Authority data for W40K-0071..W40K-0080 — a mixed late-2000s/early-2010s 40K standalone cluster: Dan Abnett's Sabbat-Worlds aviation companion novel Double Eagle (2002, Phantine pilots on Enothis), Jonathan Green's Iron Hands quest novel (2004, Iron-Father Gdolkin chasing a Ferrus-Manus relic during the 13th Black Crusade), Simon Spurrier's Night Lords noir Lord of the Night (2005, Sahaal vs Mita Ashyn on Equixus), Dan Abnett's Iron-Snakes fix-up Brothers of the Snake (2007, Brother-Sergeant Priad of Damocles Squad on Ithaka/Karybdis), C.S. Goto's controversial craftworld novel Eldar Prophecy (2007, Kaelor + Naois), Richard Williams's Imperial-Navy class-war thriller Relentless (2008, Captain Becket vs First Officer Ward + Dark Eldar), Dan Abnett's Adeptus-Titanicus ensemble piece Titanicus (2008, Orestes + Legio Invicta), Chris Roberson's Imperial-Fists recruitment novel Sons of Dorn (2010, Vernailis), Rob Sanders's Inquisitor-Czevak Webway novel Atlas Infernal (2011, Black Library + Ahriman), and Aaron Dembski-Bowden's Grey-Knights first-person novel The Emperor's Gift (2012, Hyperion + Annika Jarlsdottir + First War of Armageddon + Months of Shame). Structural notes: one format-compliance flag (W40K-0074 Brothers of the Snake is a seven-story fix-up but SSOT-roster lists novel), one low-confidence-canonicity flag for the Goto Eldar entry, and the first authority-layer entries for the Iron Hands, Iron Snakes, Imperial Fists scout/recruitment register, Adeptus Titanicus, Grey Knights, and Night Lords factions.

overrides.synopsis: Sabbat Worlds Crusade companion novel to Gaunt's Ghosts, taking the Crusade to the skies above Enothis. The elite Phantine XX fighter wing — including Commander Bree Jagdea — arrives on the desert-coast war-front in a desperate hour, with Chaos air forces driving Imperial cover off the planet and threatening to wipe out the remaining human population. Dual POV across the dogfighting line: Phantine pilots flying Thunderbolt and Marauder aircraft on one side, the infamous Chaos ace Khrel Kas Obarkon and his Hell-Talon squadrons hunting the skies on the other. Cast across both sides is large (squadron-mate ensembles, ground crew, and PDF aviator allies). A 'Battle of Britain in space' register — sustained aerial combat, attrition, pilot psychology, and the strategic stakes of holding the air corridor that the Crusade's ground forces depend on. Carries the same Sabbat-Worlds geopolitics as the Gaunt's Ghosts arc but anchors on the Imperial Navy / Phantine-aviation axis rather than the Tanith First-and-Only.

Iron-Father Gdolkin is pulled away from the defence of Medusa to honour an ancient oath sworn to the Adeptus Mechanicus, and a trail through the Mechanicus archives draws him to a long-lost world that may hold a weapon capable of turning the tide of Abaddon's 13th Black Crusade. The same lost world is rumoured to hide the resting place of Ferrus Manus, primarch of the Iron Hands — the oldest mystery of the chapter — and the quest curdles into something personal. Written from inside the chapter's machine-supremacist register: a Mechanicus-theological quest novel.

Zso Sahaal — First Captain of the VIII Legion, lost in the warp for ten thousand years since the death of his master Konrad Curze — surfaces back into realspace on the sunless hive-world of Equixus, hunting the Corona Nox: the relic he needs to reclaim his scattered legion from his treacherous rival Acerbus. Mita Ashyn, a young Inquisitorial interrogator with growing doubts about her own order's hypocrisy, is sent to Equixus on routine corruption work and finds herself on Sahaal's trail. A hive-noir told from both sides of the chase — neither cleanly hero nor villain — and a Night Lords novel where the legion's primarch-orphaned politics weigh as much as the manhunt itself.

Episodic Iron Snakes novel — seven linked tales, most first carried in the Inferno! magazine — following Brother-Sergeant Priad of Damocles Squad across roughly twelve years of duty in the Reef Stars sector. When Sergeant Raphon and two squadmates fall on an early mission, Priad inherits Raphon's legendary Lightning Claw and the squad's sergeancy; later episodes carry the squad through boarding actions, Dark Eldar slave-raid response, Ork brawls, and squad-internal reckonings of honour. Iron Snakes culture is Greek-coded — home world Ithaka, fortress-moon Karybdis, oath and hospitality and kin-debt ethics threaded through every tale.

sources: Lexicanum Brothers of the Snake (Novel); Warhammer 40k Wiki / Fandom Brothers of the Snake; Goodreads
flags.reasoning: Brothers of the Snake is structurally a fix-up of seven linked short stories (most originally published in Inferno! magazine) framed as a Priad / Damocles-Squad-progression novel over roughly twelve years. SSOT roster lists 'novel'; suggestion 'collection' or 'fix_up'. Same pattern as W40K-0025 Ghostmaker in ssot-w40k-003. Resolver brief later can decide whether to promote a 'fix_up' or 'collection' value into the format taxonomy or keep 'novel' as the umbrella.

overrides.synopsis: On the isolated craftworld Kaelor — drifting through the dark reaches, its society fragmented into quasi-feudal Houses scarred by recent House Wars and its Aspect Temples withdrawn from public life — the young warrior Naois comes of age as the unwitting subject of an old prophecy that drives him to escalating violence. Farseers, Aspect Warriors, and House lords are left to decide whether the prophecy is genuine guidance or Slaaneshi manipulation. A dense interior Eldar-politics piece rather than a wider-40K war-story.

sources: Lexicanum Eldar Prophecy (Novel); Goodreads; Black Library product page
flags.reasoning: Secondary-source coverage of Eldar Prophecy is thin and inconsistent on secondary character names (House lords, Farseers, Aspect Warrior cast, prophecy-figures) and on craftworld-internal locations beyond Kaelor itself. Per Brief 061's plot-halluzinations-disziplin, characters and locations are left tight to what summary sources consistently surface rather than invented; resolver / coverage pass later can extend if the source coverage improves.

overrides.synopsis: Class-war thriller aboard the Imperial-Navy cruiser Relentless. First Officer Ward has spent years running the ship as de-facto captain, the crew fat on illicit tithes extracted from civilian traders. When the hardliner Captain Becket is appointed above him, a conspiracy to remove Becket escalates to an assassination attempt that drops the new captain into the Conscript decks — the human fuel of an Imperial cruiser, where the strongest survive a lifetime of labour for the chance to climb back up. Becket has to work his way to the bridge to confront Ward, until a Dark Eldar raider intercept turns the internal coup into a survival fight. Ship-as-society at full scale.

Adeptus-Titanicus ensemble novel set on the vital forge-world of Orestes when a Chaos titan legion makes planetfall. Titan Legio Invicta — fresh from the front and in desperate need of refit — responds anyway, committing its surviving god-engines to the defence under the leadership of Princeps Gearhart, with Princeps Guido Prinzhorn of Legio Tempestus drafted in to crew the Titan Dominatus Victrix after most of its previous crew was killed in an Eldar engagement (only Moderati Zane Tarses survived). On the ground around the titan-line, Cally Samstag — a member of the Orestes PDF Activated 26th whose pre-war military experience was little more than weekend hikes — finds herself an unlikely junior leader as the war stretches the planetary defenders thin. Underneath the war on Orestes, archivist Tolemy and an Orestes-internal faction of the Adeptus Mechanicus surface a deeper crisis: the theological gulf between the Mechanicus (Omnissiah as separate being) and the Imperium (Omnissiah as the Emperor) opens into a real schism, and the Chaos invasion is partly cover for that internal Imperium fracture. Multi-POV doorstopper ensemble, war-story + political-thriller in roughly equal weight.

Imperial-Fists recruitment-and-training novel. The Imperial Fists, descending on a backwater planet whose technology has slipped back to roughly civil-war-era infrastructure and which has fallen out of contact with the wider Imperium, harvest some two thousand candidates from three rival nations locked in a long planetary war. Three of those candidates — Zatori, du Queste, and Taloc, drawn from opposing sides of that same local war — survive the brutal recruitment regime and progress together through the chapter's Scout-stage formation, each carrying nationally-coded grievances against the other two even as the Imperial Fists' indoctrination tries to weld them into battle-brothers. Their first deployment as fully-formed Neophytes is to Vernailis, a planet under Chaos blight, where they fight alongside their senior battle-brothers against the Roaring Blades Traitor Guard, a Chaos-corrupted Imperial Guard host. Coming-of-age / Bildungsroman structure across three POV recruits; opens what was projected as an Imperial-Fists scout trilogy.

Standalone Inquisitor-Czevak novel that compresses four crucial decades of High Inquisitor Bronislaw Czevak's life into a single chase. Czevak of the Ordo Xenos — the only human ever known to have been invited to study within the Eldar's Black Library — has stolen from there the Atlas Infernal, a living map of the Webway, and is now hunted simultaneously by three sets of pursuers: the Eldar Harlequins, sent to apprehend him and reclaim the Atlas; rivals within his own Inquisition who would rather kill him than let him keep what he has learned; and his deadliest foe, the Thousand Sons arch-sorcerer Ahzek Ahriman, who wants the knowledge inside the Black Library and inside Czevak's head to exalt himself to godhood. Webway-scale journey / cat-and-mouse pursuit across realspace and warp infrastructure, with Czevak's small allied entourage, Harlequin troupes, Thousand Sons cabals, and Inquisition strike-teams converging at every waypoint.

First-person Grey Knights novel narrated in retrospect by Hyperion of Squad Castian, covering the chapter's part in the First War of Armageddon and the intra-Imperium fallout remembered as the Months of Shame. Hyperion grows up inside Castian under Justicar Galeo as the squad's daemon-eradication work pulls them across the war and the warp around it. Annika Jarlsdottir — a Fenrisian Inquisitor whose home-world loyalty to Logan Grimnar's Space Wolves collides with Grey Knights methods — anchors the Inquisition end and brings the chapter into a later confrontation with the Wolves over the fate of Armageddon's survivors. Somber and reflective rather than action-march: incorruptible but not infallible, the moral cost is the load-bearing spine.
